channel.title: Jodie's Blog
channel.description: Personal blog by Jodie covering math, poetry, web development, and life

item.title: We Have Inkhaven at Home
item.description:
st
A friend of mine is going to this big fancy retreat called [Inkhaven](https://www.inkhaven.blog/) where you write one blog post a day. And I was like "I can't do that, but I'll sure as hell try!". I don't want to spam this blog, so I'll link my daily posts down here and I'll add the best ones to the main feed later after some polish.
[Inkhaven](https://www.inkhaven.blog/)
* April 1st - **[How to Leverage Effective Altruists to Fix Housing Prices in the Bay Area](https://morewrong.jodie.website/#how-to-leverage-effective-altruists-to-fix-housing)** - MoreWrong * April 2nd - **Animal Welfare is a Culinary Problem**
### Animal Welfare is a Culinary Problem
I agree with the vegans. Factory farming is horrific. I don't need another documentary. I'm convinced.
#### I had chicken last week.
Not because I watched it get its beak cut off and felt nothing, but because I was sick with the man flu and I wanted a comforting microwave meal, and butter chicken was all I could find.
Most restaurants have one vegetarian option that only exists so they can say they tried. If you're a normal person who wants to eat with friends, well fuck you, you're eating fries.
I cook a real meal once a day. Sometimes zero. I drink a premade protein shake instead. These shakes are load-bearing to my quality of life.
#### My body hates the obvious solution
The first thing anyone suggests when you try to eat less meat is legumes. Cheap, nutritious, available.
They also make me fart.
I've tried soaking them overnight. I've tried "just push through it, your gut adapts." My gut did not adapt. I also tried soylent, the only decent vegan meal replacement shake. Similar results. Worse than the beans actually.
#### Next time someone tells you they could never go vegan, send them a recipe.
The greatest impact I've had on animal welfare is telling people that msg makes tofu taste good. The person who's influenced me the most isn't an activist, it's [this guy on YouTube who figured out how to make vegan bacon out of rice paper](https://www.youtube.com/@SauceStache/videos).
Animal welfare organizations should take a cut of their budget and put it into food science. Vegan food needs to compete with "regular" food, not be a niche specialty product. Fix the food and you get the ethics for free.
* April 3rd - **Dust**
### Dust
the ground under your feet built from what ceased to be
each grain of sand a graveyard of forgotten stars
your body only borrows what the earth will reclaim
the dust will call you home the ground under a stranger's feet
* April 4th - **How to Make a Floating Point Math Library, Part I**
### How to Make a Floating Point Math Library, Part I
You might have seen [the fast inverse square root](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fast_inverse_square_root). The one with the magic hex constant `0x5F3759DF` and the comment _"what the fuck?"_ from the Quake III source. After reading this series, you'll understand how it works, and how to apply its arcane trickery to compute any transcendental function.
But first: what _is_ a float?
[Scientific notation](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Scientific_notation) writes a number as a power of ten, multiplied by a value between 1 and 10 called the _significand_ (or _mantissa_). Floats do the same thing in base 2: the _exponent_ stores ⌊log2(_x_)⌋, and the mantissa is between 1 and 2.
``` ┌sign┬─exponent──┬─mantissa─────────────────────┐ │ 0 │ 0000 0000 │ 000 0000 0000 0000 0000 0000 │ └────┴───────────┴──────────────────────────────┘
```
[This calculator](https://www.h-schmidt.net/FloatConverter/IEEE754.html) lets you flip individual bits and watch the value change. You'll get a feel for how different numbers are represented.
#### Manipulating Floats with Integer Operations
The upper bits of a float are proportional to the logarithm of its value. So integer math on the raw bits becomes log-domain arithmetic on the number itself.
``` log(_a_ · _b_) = log _a_ + log _b_ log(_a_ / _b_) = log _a_ − log _b_ log(_a__b_) = _b_ · log _a_ log(√_a_) = log(_a_) / 2 log(1/_a_) = −log(_a_) log(1/√_a_) = −log(_a_) / 2 ```
Addition becomes multiplication. Subtraction becomes division. A right-shift by one becomes a square root… Wait… those last two are the inverse square root!
The exponent of a number can be negative (for numbers smaller than 1). Floats handle this by storing the exponent as `exponent + 127`. The "magic" constant `0x5F3759DF` is just 2127/2. The integer subtract combines the reciprocal and the multiplication by 2127/2. Two birds, one `SUB`.
In **Part II**, we'll use this knowledge to implement other transcendental functions in code!
* April 5th - **Use Your Phone Less by Making It _More_ Fun**
### Use Your Phone Less by Making It _More_ Fun
Digital minimalism advice is usually about making your phone worse. Add friction. Greyscale the screen. Buy a flip phone.
You don't have to make your phone miserable to use. The terminal goal isn't "use your phone less." The goal is doing the things you actually want to do. You can read books on your phone, text your friends, write, navigate somewhere new. None of that is the problem. When your phone only has those there's no void to fill by scrolling.
#### Remove Your Browser
Most things you'd use a mobile browser for have an app. Single purpose, no distractions. To look something up, AI chatbots and Wikipedia search are usually better tools.
For the rare case you _need_ a browser, there's [Firefox Focus](https://www.mozilla.org/en-CA/firefox/browsers/mobile/focus/). When you close it, it wipes everything. No start page, no autocomplete, no history. You can only go on websites intentionally. It doesn't keep you logged in either. Every addictive platform requires a login. No login, no slop.
#### And it Works?
Now when I pick up my phone reflexively to fill time, there's nothing to scroll. I put it down. Or I text a friend, read a book, write a blog post. No willpower. No discomfort. I never regret being on my phone.
* April 1st - **[How to Leverage Effective Altruists to Fix Housing Prices in the Bay Area](https://morewrong.jodie.website/#how-to-leverage-effective-altruists-to-fix-housing)** - MoreWrong st**[How to Leverage Effective Altruists to Fix Housing Prices in the Bay Area](https://morewrong.jodie.website/#how-to-leverage-effective-altruists-to-fix-housing)**[How to Leverage Effective Altruists to Fix Housing Prices in the Bay Area](https://morewrong.jodie.website/#how-to-leverage-effective-altruists-to-fix-housing)* April 2nd - **Animal Welfare is a Culinary Problem**
### Animal Welfare is a Culinary Problem
I agree with the vegans. Factory farming is horrific. I don't need another documentary. I'm convinced.
#### I had chicken last week.
Not because I watched it get its beak cut off and felt nothing, but because I was sick with the man flu and I wanted a comforting microwave meal, and butter chicken was all I could find.
Most restaurants have one vegetarian option that only exists so they can say they tried. If you're a normal person who wants to eat with friends, well fuck you, you're eating fries.
I cook a real meal once a day. Sometimes zero. I drink a premade protein shake instead. These shakes are load-bearing to my quality of life.
#### My body hates the obvious solution
The first thing anyone suggests when you try to eat less meat is legumes. Cheap, nutritious, available.
They also make me fart.
I've tried soaking them overnight. I've tried "just push through it, your gut adapts." My gut did not adapt. I also tried soylent, the only decent vegan meal replacement shake. Similar results. Worse than the beans actually.
#### Next time someone tells you they could never go vegan, send them a recipe.
The greatest impact I've had on animal welfare is telling people that msg makes tofu taste good. The person who's influenced me the most isn't an activist, it's [this guy on YouTube who figured out how to make vegan bacon out of rice paper](https://www.youtube.com/@SauceStache/videos).
Animal welfare organizations should take a cut of their budget and put it into food science. Vegan food needs to compete with "regular" food, not be a niche specialty product. Fix the food and you get the ethics for free.
April 2nd - **Animal Welfare is a Culinary Problem**
### Animal Welfare is a Culinary Problem
I agree with the vegans. Factory farming is horrific. I don't need another documentary. I'm convinced.
#### I had chicken last week.
Not because I watched it get its beak cut off and felt nothing, but because I was sick with the man flu and I wanted a comforting microwave meal, and butter chicken was all I could find.
Most restaurants have one vegetarian option that only exists so they can say they tried. If you're a normal person who wants to eat with friends, well fuck you, you're eating fries.
I cook a real meal once a day. Sometimes zero. I drink a premade protein shake instead. These shakes are load-bearing to my quality of life.
#### My body hates the obvious solution
The first thing anyone suggests when you try to eat less meat is legumes. Cheap, nutritious, available.
They also make me fart.
I've tried soaking them overnight. I've tried "just push through it, your gut adapts." My gut did not adapt. I also tried soylent, the only decent vegan meal replacement shake. Similar results. Worse than the beans actually.
#### Next time someone tells you they could never go vegan, send them a recipe.
The greatest impact I've had on animal welfare is telling people that msg makes tofu taste good. The person who's influenced me the most isn't an activist, it's [this guy on YouTube who figured out how to make vegan bacon out of rice paper](https://www.youtube.com/@SauceStache/videos).
Animal welfare organizations should take a cut of their budget and put it into food science. Vegan food needs to compete with "regular" food, not be a niche specialty product. Fix the food and you get the ethics for free.
April 2nd - **Animal Welfare is a Culinary Problem**nd**Animal Welfare is a Culinary Problem**
### Animal Welfare is a Culinary Problem
I agree with the vegans. Factory farming is horrific. I don't need another documentary. I'm convinced.
#### I had chicken last week.
Not because I watched it get its beak cut off and felt nothing, but because I was sick with the man flu and I wanted a comforting microwave meal, and butter chicken was all I could find.
Most restaurants have one vegetarian option that only exists so they can say they tried. If you're a normal person who wants to eat with friends, well fuck you, you're eating fries.
I cook a real meal once a day. Sometimes zero. I drink a premade protein shake instead. These shakes are load-bearing to my quality of life.
#### My body hates the obvious solution
The first thing anyone suggests when you try to eat less meat is legumes. Cheap, nutritious, available.
They also make me fart.
I've tried soaking them overnight. I've tried "just push through it, your gut adapts." My gut did not adapt. I also tried soylent, the only decent vegan meal replacement shake. Similar results. Worse than the beans actually.
#### Next time someone tells you they could never go vegan, send them a recipe.
The greatest impact I've had on animal welfare is telling people that msg makes tofu taste good. The person who's influenced me the most isn't an activist, it's [this guy on YouTube who figured out how to make vegan bacon out of rice paper](https://www.youtube.com/@SauceStache/videos).
Animal welfare organizations should take a cut of their budget and put it into food science. Vegan food needs to compete with "regular" food, not be a niche specialty product. Fix the food and you get the ethics for free.
I agree with the vegans. Factory farming is horrific. I don't need another documentary. I'm convinced.
#### I had chicken last week.
Not because I watched it get its beak cut off and felt nothing, but because I was sick with the man flu and I wanted a comforting microwave meal, and butter chicken was all I could find.
Most restaurants have one vegetarian option that only exists so they can say they tried. If you're a normal person who wants to eat with friends, well fuck you, you're eating fries.
I cook a real meal once a day. Sometimes zero. I drink a premade protein shake instead. These shakes are load-bearing to my quality of life.
#### My body hates the obvious solution
The first thing anyone suggests when you try to eat less meat is legumes. Cheap, nutritious, available.
They also make me fart.
I've tried soaking them overnight. I've tried "just push through it, your gut adapts." My gut did not adapt. I also tried soylent, the only decent vegan meal replacement shake. Similar results. Worse than the beans actually.
#### Next time someone tells you they could never go vegan, send them a recipe.
The greatest impact I've had on animal welfare is telling people that msg makes tofu taste good. The person who's influenced me the most isn't an activist, it's [this guy on YouTube who figured out how to make vegan bacon out of rice paper](https://www.youtube.com/@SauceStache/videos).
msg[this guy on YouTube who figured out how to make vegan bacon out of rice paper](https://www.youtube.com/@SauceStache/videos)
Animal welfare organizations should take a cut of their budget and put it into food science. Vegan food needs to compete with "regular" food, not be a niche specialty product. Fix the food and you get the ethics for free.
* April 3rd - **Dust**
### Dust
the ground under your feet built from what ceased to be
each grain of sand a graveyard of forgotten stars
your body only borrows what the earth will reclaim
the dust will call you home the ground under a stranger's feet
April 3rd - **Dust**
### Dust
the ground under your feet built from what ceased to be
each grain of sand a graveyard of forgotten stars
your body only borrows what the earth will reclaim
the dust will call you home the ground under a stranger's feet
April 3rd - **Dust**rd**Dust**
### Dust
the ground under your feet built from what ceased to be
each grain of sand a graveyard of forgotten stars
your body only borrows what the earth will reclaim
the dust will call you home the ground under a stranger's feet
the ground under your feet built from what ceased to be
each grain of sand a graveyard of forgotten stars
your body only borrows what the earth will reclaim
the dust will call you home the ground under a stranger's feet
* April 4th - **How to Make a Floating Point Math Library, Part I**
### How to Make a Floating Point Math Library, Part I
You might have seen [the fast inverse square root](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fast_inverse_square_root). The one with the magic hex constant `0x5F3759DF` and the comment _"what the fuck?"_ from the Quake III source. After reading this series, you'll understand how it works, and how to apply its arcane trickery to compute any transcendental function.
But first: what _is_ a float?
[Scientific notation](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Scientific_notation) writes a number as a power of ten, multiplied by a value between 1 and 10 called the _significand_ (or _mantissa_). Floats do the same thing in base 2: the _exponent_ stores ⌊log2(_x_)⌋, and the mantissa is between 1 and 2.
``` ┌sign┬─exponent──┬─mantissa─────────────────────┐ │ 0 │ 0000 0000 │ 000 0000 0000 0000 0000 0000 │ └────┴───────────┴──────────────────────────────┘
```
[This calculator](https://www.h-schmidt.net/FloatConverter/IEEE754.html) lets you flip individual bits and watch the value change. You'll get a feel for how different numbers are represented.
#### Manipulating Floats with Integer Operations
The upper bits of a float are proportional to the logarithm of its value. So integer math on the raw bits becomes log-domain arithmetic on the number itself.
``` log(_a_ · _b_) = log _a_ + log _b_ log(_a_ / _b_) = log _a_ − log _b_ log(_a__b_) = _b_ · log _a_ log(√_a_) = log(_a_) / 2 log(1/_a_) = −log(_a_) log(1/√_a_) = −log(_a_) / 2 ```
Addition becomes multiplication. Subtraction becomes division. A right-shift by one becomes a square root… Wait… those last two are the inverse square root!
The exponent of a number can be negative (for numbers smaller than 1). Floats handle this by storing the exponent as `exponent + 127`. The "magic" constant `0x5F3759DF` is just 2127/2. The integer subtract combines the reciprocal and the multiplication by 2127/2. Two birds, one `SUB`.
In **Part II**, we'll use this knowledge to implement other transcendental functions in code!
April 4th - **How to Make a Floating Point Math Library, Part I**
### How to Make a Floating Point Math Library, Part I
You might have seen [the fast inverse square root](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fast_inverse_square_root). The one with the magic hex constant `0x5F3759DF` and the comment _"what the fuck?"_ from the Quake III source. After reading this series, you'll understand how it works, and how to apply its arcane trickery to compute any transcendental function.
But first: what _is_ a float?
[Scientific notation](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Scientific_notation) writes a number as a power of ten, multiplied by a value between 1 and 10 called the _significand_ (or _mantissa_). Floats do the same thing in base 2: the _exponent_ stores ⌊log2(_x_)⌋, and the mantissa is between 1 and 2.
``` ┌sign┬─exponent──┬─mantissa─────────────────────┐ │ 0 │ 0000 0000 │ 000 0000 0000 0000 0000 0000 │ └────┴───────────┴──────────────────────────────┘
```
[This calculator](https://www.h-schmidt.net/FloatConverter/IEEE754.html) lets you flip individual bits and watch the value change. You'll get a feel for how different numbers are represented.
#### Manipulating Floats with Integer Operations
The upper bits of a float are proportional to the logarithm of its value. So integer math on the raw bits becomes log-domain arithmetic on the number itself.
``` log(_a_ · _b_) = log _a_ + log _b_ log(_a_ / _b_) = log _a_ − log _b_ log(_a__b_) = _b_ · log _a_ log(√_a_) = log(_a_) / 2 log(1/_a_) = −log(_a_) log(1/√_a_) = −log(_a_) / 2 ```
Addition becomes multiplication. Subtraction becomes division. A right-shift by one becomes a square root… Wait… those last two are the inverse square root!
The exponent of a number can be negative (for numbers smaller than 1). Floats handle this by storing the exponent as `exponent + 127`. The "magic" constant `0x5F3759DF` is just 2127/2. The integer subtract combines the reciprocal and the multiplication by 2127/2. Two birds, one `SUB`.
In **Part II**, we'll use this knowledge to implement other transcendental functions in code!
April 4th - **How to Make a Floating Point Math Library, Part I**th**How to Make a Floating Point Math Library, Part I**
### How to Make a Floating Point Math Library, Part I
You might have seen [the fast inverse square root](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fast_inverse_square_root). The one with the magic hex constant `0x5F3759DF` and the comment _"what the fuck?"_ from the Quake III source. After reading this series, you'll understand how it works, and how to apply its arcane trickery to compute any transcendental function.
But first: what _is_ a float?
[Scientific notation](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Scientific_notation) writes a number as a power of ten, multiplied by a value between 1 and 10 called the _significand_ (or _mantissa_). Floats do the same thing in base 2: the _exponent_ stores ⌊log2(_x_)⌋, and the mantissa is between 1 and 2.
``` ┌sign┬─exponent──┬─mantissa─────────────────────┐ │ 0 │ 0000 0000 │ 000 0000 0000 0000 0000 0000 │ └────┴───────────┴──────────────────────────────┘
```
[This calculator](https://www.h-schmidt.net/FloatConverter/IEEE754.html) lets you flip individual bits and watch the value change. You'll get a feel for how different numbers are represented.
#### Manipulating Floats with Integer Operations
The upper bits of a float are proportional to the logarithm of its value. So integer math on the raw bits becomes log-domain arithmetic on the number itself.
``` log(_a_ · _b_) = log _a_ + log _b_ log(_a_ / _b_) = log _a_ − log _b_ log(_a__b_) = _b_ · log _a_ log(√_a_) = log(_a_) / 2 log(1/_a_) = −log(_a_) log(1/√_a_) = −log(_a_) / 2 ```
Addition becomes multiplication. Subtraction becomes division. A right-shift by one becomes a square root… Wait… those last two are the inverse square root!
The exponent of a number can be negative (for numbers smaller than 1). Floats handle this by storing the exponent as `exponent + 127`. The "magic" constant `0x5F3759DF` is just 2127/2. The integer subtract combines the reciprocal and the multiplication by 2127/2. Two birds, one `SUB`.
In **Part II**, we'll use this knowledge to implement other transcendental functions in code!
You might have seen [the fast inverse square root](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fast_inverse_square_root). The one with the magic hex constant `0x5F3759DF` and the comment _"what the fuck?"_ from the Quake III source. After reading this series, you'll understand how it works, and how to apply its arcane trickery to compute any transcendental function.
[the fast inverse square root](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fast_inverse_square_root)`0x5F3759DF`_"what the fuck?"_
But first: what _is_ a float?
_is_
[Scientific notation](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Scientific_notation) writes a number as a power of ten, multiplied by a value between 1 and 10 called the _significand_ (or _mantissa_). Floats do the same thing in base 2: the _exponent_ stores ⌊log2(_x_)⌋, and the mantissa is between 1 and 2.
[Scientific notation](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Scientific_notation)_significand__mantissa__exponent_2_x_``` ┌sign┬─exponent──┬─mantissa─────────────────────┐ │ 0 │ 0000 0000 │ 000 0000 0000 0000 0000 0000 │ └────┴───────────┴──────────────────────────────┘
``` `┌sign┬─exponent──┬─mantissa─────────────────────┐ │ 0 │ 0000 0000 │ 000 0000 0000 0000 0000 0000 │ └────┴───────────┴──────────────────────────────┘`
[This calculator](https://www.h-schmidt.net/FloatConverter/IEEE754.html) lets you flip individual bits and watch the value change. You'll get a feel for how different numbers are represented.
[This calculator](https://www.h-schmidt.net/FloatConverter/IEEE754.html)
#### Manipulating Floats with Integer Operations
The upper bits of a float are proportional to the logarithm of its value. So integer math on the raw bits becomes log-domain arithmetic on the number itself.
``` log(_a_ · _b_) = log _a_ + log _b_ log(_a_ / _b_) = log _a_ − log _b_ log(_a__b_) = _b_ · log _a_ log(√_a_) = log(_a_) / 2 log(1/_a_) = −log(_a_) log(1/√_a_) = −log(_a_) / 2 ``` `log(_a_ · _b_) = log _a_ + log _b_ log(_a_ / _b_) = log _a_ − log _b_ log(_a__b_) = _b_ · log _a_ log(√_a_) = log(_a_) / 2 log(1/_a_) = −log(_a_) log(1/√_a_) = −log(_a_) / 2`_a__b__a__b__a__b__a__b__a__b__b__b__a__a__a__a__a__a__a_
Addition becomes multiplication. Subtraction becomes division. A right-shift by one becomes a square root… Wait… those last two are the inverse square root!
The exponent of a number can be negative (for numbers smaller than 1). Floats handle this by storing the exponent as `exponent + 127`. The "magic" constant `0x5F3759DF` is just 2127/2. The integer subtract combines the reciprocal and the multiplication by 2127/2. Two birds, one `SUB`.
`exponent + 127``0x5F3759DF`127/2127/2`SUB`
In **Part II**, we'll use this knowledge to implement other transcendental functions in code!
**Part II*** April 5th - **Use Your Phone Less by Making It _More_ Fun**
### Use Your Phone Less by Making It _More_ Fun
Digital minimalism advice is usually about making your phone worse. Add friction. Greyscale the screen. Buy a flip phone.
You don't have to make your phone miserable to use. The terminal goal isn't "use your phone less." The goal is doing the things you actually want to do. You can read books on your phone, text your friends, write, navigate somewhere new. None of that is the problem. When your phone only has those there's no void to fill by scrolling.
#### Remove Your Browser
Most things you'd use a mobile browser for have an app. Single purpose, no distractions. To look something up, AI chatbots and Wikipedia search are usually better tools.
For the rare case you _need_ a browser, there's [Firefox Focus](https://www.mozilla.org/en-CA/firefox/browsers/mobile/focus/). When you close it, it wipes everything. No start page, no autocomplete, no history. You can only go on websites intentionally. It doesn't keep you logged in either. Every addictive platform requires a login. No login, no slop.
#### And it Works?
Now when I pick up my phone reflexively to fill time, there's nothing to scroll. I put it down. Or I text a friend, read a book, write a blog post. No willpower. No discomfort. I never regret being on my phone.
April 5th - **Use Your Phone Less by Making It _More_ Fun**
### Use Your Phone Less by Making It _More_ Fun
Digital minimalism advice is usually about making your phone worse. Add friction. Greyscale the screen. Buy a flip phone.
You don't have to make your phone miserable to use. The terminal goal isn't "use your phone less." The goal is doing the things you actually want to do. You can read books on your phone, text your friends, write, navigate somewhere new. None of that is the problem. When your phone only has those there's no void to fill by scrolling.
#### Remove Your Browser
Most things you'd use a mobile browser for have an app. Single purpose, no distractions. To look something up, AI chatbots and Wikipedia search are usually better tools.
For the rare case you _need_ a browser, there's [Firefox Focus](https://www.mozilla.org/en-CA/firefox/browsers/mobile/focus/). When you close it, it wipes everything. No start page, no autocomplete, no history. You can only go on websites intentionally. It doesn't keep you logged in either. Every addictive platform requires a login. No login, no slop.
#### And it Works?
Now when I pick up my phone reflexively to fill time, there's nothing to scroll. I put it down. Or I text a friend, read a book, write a blog post. No willpower. No discomfort. I never regret being on my phone.
April 5th - **Use Your Phone Less by Making It _More_ Fun**th**Use Your Phone Less by Making It _More_ Fun**_More_
### Use Your Phone Less by Making It _More_ Fun
Digital minimalism advice is usually about making your phone worse. Add friction. Greyscale the screen. Buy a flip phone.
You don't have to make your phone miserable to use. The terminal goal isn't "use your phone less." The goal is doing the things you actually want to do. You can read books on your phone, text your friends, write, navigate somewhere new. None of that is the problem. When your phone only has those there's no void to fill by scrolling.
#### Remove Your Browser
Most things you'd use a mobile browser for have an app. Single purpose, no distractions. To look something up, AI chatbots and Wikipedia search are usually better tools.
For the rare case you _need_ a browser, there's [Firefox Focus](https://www.mozilla.org/en-CA/firefox/browsers/mobile/focus/). When you close it, it wipes everything. No start page, no autocomplete, no history. You can only go on websites intentionally. It doesn't keep you logged in either. Every addictive platform requires a login. No login, no slop.
#### And it Works?
Now when I pick up my phone reflexively to fill time, there's nothing to scroll. I put it down. Or I text a friend, read a book, write a blog post. No willpower. No discomfort. I never regret being on my phone.
_More_
Digital minimalism advice is usually about making your phone worse. Add friction. Greyscale the screen. Buy a flip phone.
You don't have to make your phone miserable to use. The terminal goal isn't "use your phone less." The goal is doing the things you actually want to do. You can read books on your phone, text your friends, write, navigate somewhere new. None of that is the problem. When your phone only has those there's no void to fill by scrolling.
#### Remove Your Browser
Most things you'd use a mobile browser for have an app. Single purpose, no distractions. To look something up, AI chatbots and Wikipedia search are usually better tools.
For the rare case you _need_ a browser, there's [Firefox Focus](https://www.mozilla.org/en-CA/firefox/browsers/mobile/focus/). When you close it, it wipes everything. No start page, no autocomplete, no history. You can only go on websites intentionally. It doesn't keep you logged in either. Every addictive platform requires a login. No login, no slop.
_need_[Firefox Focus](https://www.mozilla.org/en-CA/firefox/browsers/mobile/focus/)
#### And it Works?
Now when I pick up my phone reflexively to fill time, there's nothing to scroll. I put it down. Or I text a friend, read a book, write a blog post. No willpower. No discomfort. I never regret being on my phone.
item.dc:creator: Jodie

item.title: Animal Welfare is a Culinary Problem
item.description:
I agree with the vegans. Factory farming is horrific. I don't need another documentary. I'm convinced.
#### I had chicken last week.
Not because I watched it get its beak cut off and felt nothing, but because I was sick with the man flu and I wanted a comforting microwave meal, and butter chicken was all I could find.
Most restaurants have one vegetarian option that only exists so they can say they tried. If you're a normal person who wants to eat with friends, well fuck you, you're eating fries.
I cook a real meal once a day. Sometimes zero. I drink a premade protein shake instead. These shakes are load-bearing to my quality of life.
#### My body hates the obvious solution
The first thing anyone suggests when you try to eat less meat is legumes. Cheap, nutritious, available.
They also make me fart.
I've tried soaking them overnight. I've tried "just push through it, your gut adapts." My gut did not adapt. I also tried soylent, the only decent vegan meal replacement shake. Similar results. Worse than the beans actually.
#### Next time someone tells you they could never go vegan, send them a recipe.
The greatest impact I've had on animal welfare is telling people that msg makes tofu taste good. The person who's influenced me the most isn't an activist, it's [this guy on YouTube who figured out how to make vegan bacon out of rice paper](https://www.youtube.com/@SauceStache/videos).
msg[this guy on YouTube who figured out how to make vegan bacon out of rice paper](https://www.youtube.com/@SauceStache/videos)
Animal welfare organizations should take a cut of their budget and put it into food science. Vegan food needs to compete with "regular" food, not be a niche specialty product. Fix the food and you get the ethics for free.
item.dc:creator: Jodie

item.title: Dust
item.description:
the ground under your feet built from what ceased to be
each grain of sand a graveyard of forgotten stars
your body only borrows what the earth will reclaim
the dust will call you home the ground under a stranger's feet
item.dc:creator: Jodie

item.title: How to Make a Floating Point Math Library, Part I
item.description:
You might have seen [the fast inverse square root](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fast_inverse_square_root). The one with the magic hex constant `0x5F3759DF` and the comment _"what the fuck?"_ from the Quake III source. After reading this series, you'll understand how it works, and how to apply its arcane trickery to compute any transcendental function.
[the fast inverse square root](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fast_inverse_square_root)`0x5F3759DF`_"what the fuck?"_
But first: what _is_ a float?
_is_
[Scientific notation](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Scientific_notation) writes a number as a power of ten, multiplied by a value between 1 and 10 called the _significand_ (or _mantissa_). Floats do the same thing in base 2: the _exponent_ stores ⌊log2(_x_)⌋, and the mantissa is between 1 and 2.
[Scientific notation](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Scientific_notation)_significand__mantissa__exponent_2_x_``` ┌sign┬─exponent──┬─mantissa─────────────────────┐ │ 0 │ 0000 0000 │ 000 0000 0000 0000 0000 0000 │ └────┴───────────┴──────────────────────────────┘
``` `┌sign┬─exponent──┬─mantissa─────────────────────┐ │ 0 │ 0000 0000 │ 000 0000 0000 0000 0000 0000 │ └────┴───────────┴──────────────────────────────┘`
[This calculator](https://www.h-schmidt.net/FloatConverter/IEEE754.html) lets you flip individual bits and watch the value change. You'll get a feel for how different numbers are represented.
[This calculator](https://www.h-schmidt.net/FloatConverter/IEEE754.html)
#### Manipulating Floats with Integer Operations
The upper bits of a float are proportional to the logarithm of its value. So integer math on the raw bits becomes log-domain arithmetic on the number itself.
``` log(_a_ · _b_) = log _a_ + log _b_ log(_a_ / _b_) = log _a_ − log _b_ log(_a__b_) = _b_ · log _a_ log(√_a_) = log(_a_) / 2 log(1/_a_) = −log(_a_) log(1/√_a_) = −log(_a_) / 2 ``` `log(_a_ · _b_) = log _a_ + log _b_ log(_a_ / _b_) = log _a_ − log _b_ log(_a__b_) = _b_ · log _a_ log(√_a_) = log(_a_) / 2 log(1/_a_) = −log(_a_) log(1/√_a_) = −log(_a_) / 2`_a__b__a__b__a__b__a__b__a__b__b__b__a__a__a__a__a__a__a_
Addition becomes multiplication. Subtraction becomes division. A right-shift by one becomes a square root… Wait… those last two are the inverse square root!
The exponent of a number can be negative (for numbers smaller than 1). Floats handle this by storing the exponent as `exponent + 127`. The "magic" constant `0x5F3759DF` is just 2127/2. The integer subtract combines the reciprocal and the multiplication by 2127/2. Two birds, one `SUB`.
`exponent + 127``0x5F3759DF`127/2127/2`SUB`
In **Part II**, we'll use this knowledge to implement other transcendental functions in code!
**Part II**
item.dc:creator: Jodie

item.title: Use Your Phone Less by Making It More Fun
item.description:
_More_
Digital minimalism advice is usually about making your phone worse. Add friction. Greyscale the screen. Buy a flip phone.
You don't have to make your phone miserable to use. The terminal goal isn't "use your phone less." The goal is doing the things you actually want to do. You can read books on your phone, text your friends, write, navigate somewhere new. None of that is the problem. When your phone only has those there's no void to fill by scrolling.
#### Remove Your Browser
Most things you'd use a mobile browser for have an app. Single purpose, no distractions. To look something up, AI chatbots and Wikipedia search are usually better tools.
For the rare case you _need_ a browser, there's [Firefox Focus](https://www.mozilla.org/en-CA/firefox/browsers/mobile/focus/). When you close it, it wipes everything. No start page, no autocomplete, no history. You can only go on websites intentionally. It doesn't keep you logged in either. Every addictive platform requires a login. No login, no slop.
_need_[Firefox Focus](https://www.mozilla.org/en-CA/firefox/browsers/mobile/focus/)
#### And it Works?
Now when I pick up my phone reflexively to fill time, there's nothing to scroll. I put it down. Or I text a friend, read a book, write a blog post. No willpower. No discomfort. I never regret being on my phone.
item.dc:creator: Jodie

item.title: Gray
item.description:
th
we're different types of gray
mine is jagged concrete yours a calm rainy morning
people always say we match
but gray is just the way colors look when they're all blended together
item.dc:creator: Jodie

item.title: The Hum of the Refrigerator
item.description:
th
#### The Farmers
Meta makes about $50 per user per year. Google makes about $70. You are not paying for these products, advertisers are. And those advertisers make even MORE money. Big Tech doesn't make the product good for _you_. They make it good for advertisers, advertisers who've gotten frighteningly good at exploiting your weaknesses.
_you_
#### The Pasture
Some software exists because someone thought it should. Some is optimized to be so delightful you gladly pay for it. Some forums let you talk about what you chose to talk about, not whatever leads to more "engagement". None of these are designed to _take_ from you. They're made to _provide_.
_take__provide_
#### Escaping the Farm
I didn't realize how much targeted advertising and algorithmically optimized engagement maximization wore me down until I stopped using platforms that do it. It's like noticing the hum of the refrigerator only when it stops.
Now I feel like I decide for myself what I want to do and then I do it. If I fall into an engaging rabbit hole, I'm not following a path designed to extract maximal engagement from me. I'm following my own curiosity. I'm _doing_ something instead of passively observing adversarial input that generates addictive chemicals in my brain. I feel a sense of achievement instead of feeling like I've wasted my time.
_doing_
item.dc:creator: Jodie

item.title: Solstice Talk
item.description:
th
— _Read at the 2025 [Secular Solstice](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Secular_Solstice) celebration_
_Read at the 2025 [Secular Solstice](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Secular_Solstice) celebration_[Secular Solstice](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Secular_Solstice)
At first glance humans aren't very equipped for survival. We have no claws to defend ourselves, no fur to protect us from the cold and the sun. But what we have is one unique trait that allowed us to thrive: imagining the future.
Through predictive modelling, long term planning, and a fair bit of wishful thinking, we didn't just hope a better world was possible, we were able to execute on our plans and mould the world into the shape of our fantasies.
We imagined a world where a mother didn't have to watch her child die of smallpox… and then we made that world real. Our ancestors looked at the sky and imagined touching it. And their grandchildren left footprints on the moon.
Most of our problems are a failure of imagination. If we can only imagine dystopia, how can we plan for utopia? We have a moral duty to use our powers to imagine futures that should exist.
Tonight, on this darkest day, we do what our ancestors did: we gather, we speculate, and we steal warmth from the future. May the worlds we conjure tonight be the ones we build tomorrow.
item.dc:creator: Jodie

item.title: The Accelerationist Luddite
item.description:
th
#### Why Be an [Accelerationist](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Accelerationism)?
[Accelerationist](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Accelerationism)
I don't know the exact risk from artificial superintelligence, and neither does anyone else. But I do know the risk of doing nothing: disease, poverty, war, pollution, worms that burrow into people's eyeballs. Technology is for fixing things. Faster. Cheaper. For more people. I want new drugs discovered in weeks, not years, because the patient doesn’t have years.
You think acceleration is reckless? I think it’s responsible. Responsibility is seeing pain and moving toward it with urgency.
#### Why Be a [Luddite](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Luddite)?
[Luddite](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Luddite)
We are building the wrong things faster than ever. We have ten thousand apps to track our sleep because we are too overstimulated to sleep. We have apps that stop you from using your other apps. My washing machine has a touch screen, it talks, and it has "ai", and yet I still have to empty the lint trap by hand.
ai
That's not progress! The wheel. Writing. Soap. That's real progress.
Silicon Valley convinced us that the price of convenience is living in their panopticon. They call us "users", a word for drug addicts. All "technology" eventually falls to [enshittification](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Enshittification).
[enshittification](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Enshittification)
The point of saving time is to have time. The point of productivity is to produce _something that matters_. We don't have time, we have screen time. We don't produce things, we produce value for the shareholders.
_something that matters_
#### Por que no los dos?
![Why not both?](https://static2.mtlws.ca/whynot.jpg)
![Why not both?](https://static2.mtlws.ca/whynot.jpg)
I don’t hate technology. I hate technology that hates me back.
I want tools that accomplish their purpose and then _shut the fuck up_. A hammer disappears in your hand. A road disappears under your feet. I don't want to be inside the machine. I don't want to be the machine. I want to do human things, in a world my brain evolved to comprehend. I want robots in the fields so I can tend to my beautiful garden. I want a society that is fast, optimised and precise in its infrastructure, so it can be slow, inefficient and analog in its daily life.
_shut the fuck up_
The real divide isn't between technophiles and technophobes; it's between technology that serves human flourishing and technology that flattens the human spirit. Every tool can also be a weapon. We can use the same hammer to build the future we want, and destroy the future we don't want.
![and the crowd cheers](https://static2.mtlws.ca/both.jpg)
![and the crowd cheers](https://static2.mtlws.ca/both.jpg)
item.dc:creator: Jodie

item.title: The Case for Single-Purpose Devices
item.description:
th
> _My own behavior baffles me. I find myself doing what I hate, and not doing what I really want to do!_ > ― Saint Paul
_My own behavior baffles me. I find myself doing what I hate, and not doing what I really want to do!_
The smartphone isn't the ultimate tool. It isn't even _a_ tool. It's _all_ the tools.
_a__all_
#### You Aren't the Main Character
If you have a book, you read. If you have a camera, you take pictures. If you have a Game Boy, you play a game. If you have all of those crammed into a phone, you don’t do any of that. You scroll TikTok.
When you pick up a book, you’ve already decided to read. When you pick up your phone, you’ve decided nothing. The phone decides for you.
#### Swiss Army Knives
Smartphones didn’t improve on what came before. They’re worse books, worse game consoles, worse computers, worse music players. Their only advantage is fitting all this mediocrity in your pocket.
Almost everyone owns a kitchen knife, tweezers, a screwdriver. Nobody thinks a Swiss Army knife is an adequate replacement, so why do we all torture ourselves with smartphones?
#### Smartphones Aren't the Entire Future
Single-purpose devices are making a quiet comeback because they never lost. Every tech product got better and cheaper while you weren't looking: flashlights, digital watches, e-readers, laptops, cameras.
#### [Chesterton](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/G._K._Chesterton#Chesterton's_fence)'s Turntable
[Chesterton](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/G._K._Chesterton#Chesterton's_fence)
Vinyls and Polaroids aren't just back because of nostalgia. "Better" technology was missing something important. Vinyl records encourage active listening. Instant cameras don't post to Instagram and ruin your opsec. Often newer technology is a different trade-off, not an outright improvement.
opsec
After 15 years of going full phone. I'm gradually reintroducing the things I thought it killed. I'm buying and borrowing books again. Books are so nice to read compared to a screen, I love them! I've been carrying a paper notepad and a pen again. I love how it allows me to take notes while talking to someone, during classes, or at church without looking rude by pulling out my phone. Paper is freeing. I can draw and highlight and annotate with no friction. It's the better tool for the job.
#### Whimsy
Looking at your phone is like living in a beige drywall box. It's efficient, it's "modern", but wouldn't you rather live in a picturesque 100-year-old cottage? Isn't reading a book under a tree more romantic than scrolling Twitter while you're sitting on the toilet? Respect yourself! Don't wear the grey jumpsuit! Don't eat the protein cube!
item.dc:creator: Jodie

item.title: Rational Black-body
item.dc:creator: Jodie

item.title: Untitled
item.dc:creator: Jodie

item.title: Time isn't real man
item.description:
th
> _There is nothing more deceptive than an obvious fact._ > ― Arthur Conan Doyle
_There is nothing more deceptive than an obvious fact._
Anyone who's pulled an all-nighter, been bored, or fallen into a YouTube rabbit hole knows that time is subjective. Brains aren't clocks. They cheat constantly.
#### Code Monks
Programmers enter multi-hour flow states without fatigue or hunger. Usually helped by autism headphones and chemical stimulants.
#### Bullet Time
People in life-or-death situations often report time slowing down. The brain temporarily overclocks and hyper-saturates itself with sensory input.
#### Teleportation
Notice how commuting to your job feels like a time skip? Mundane regular tasks don't exist to us.
#### Time Travel
When you travel, a few weeks turn into a whole-ass chapter of your life. A giant exotic memory palace. At the end you often feel like you're leaving an entire life behind.
#### Time Merchants
Video platforms optimize for smoothness and hyperstimulus. They make you forget where videos start and end.
#### Chronohacking
Time perception is hackable. It's not just for fun, it feels _necessary_ in a world where attention is farmed and sold. There's only so much we can do to have a longer life, so why not make our life _denser_.
_necessary__denser_
item.dc:creator: Jodie

item.title: Graphics Optimization is Information Theory
item.description:
th
I've been working on an image compression format lately (coming soon!). This led me down the rabbit hole of [information theory](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Information_theory), and it's made me see graphics programming with a fresh perspective.
[information theory](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Information_theory)
Information theory is too often overlooked in the context of graphics optimization. It’s not just adjacent to what we do. It's the whole darn [map](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Map%E2%80%93territory_relation)!
[map](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Map%E2%80%93territory_relation)
#### Compute Generates Data
Redundancy can be compressed. This principle applies to compute, not just storage.
#### Less Data Also Matters
Low-end hardware is usually bottlenecked by memory bandwidth, not compute. Phones and igpu powered laptops also tend to have less vram!
igpu
Making data smaller also makes space for something else, like lookup tables or acceleration structures that would be unviable otherwise
#### Where?
* **Temporal Redundancy:** The current frame is often similar to the previous one. Reprojection, taa, and impostors exploit this to reduce work. * **Derived Data:** Don’t store what you can calculate. Like normals and positions from depth. * **Spatial Redundancy:** Adjacent pixels are often the same material and are similarly lit. * **Impossible States:** If the camera can’t possibly see it. Cull it.
* **Temporal Redundancy:** The current frame is often similar to the previous one. Reprojection, taa, and impostors exploit this to reduce work. **Temporal Redundancy:**taa* **Derived Data:** Don’t store what you can calculate. Like normals and positions from depth. **Derived Data:*** **Spatial Redundancy:** Adjacent pixels are often the same material and are similarly lit. **Spatial Redundancy:*** **Impossible States:** If the camera can’t possibly see it. Cull it. **Impossible States:**
#### Human Perception has a Limited Bitrate
If there's more of one type of information, you can perceive less of another.
* **High Frequency, High Contrast Detail:** Allows for lower bit depth especially for chroma. * **High Motion:** Lower resolution is fine. * **Low Motion:** More temporal redundancy. * **More Pixels and More Frames:** Temporal tricks and dithering shine at high framerate and resolution. * **Blur:** Rough reflections and depth of field need more rays or more texture samples, but you can use a lower resolution for raytracing and texture lookups.
* **High Frequency, High Contrast Detail:** Allows for lower bit depth especially for chroma. **High Frequency, High Contrast Detail:*** **High Motion:** Lower resolution is fine. **High Motion:*** **Low Motion:** More temporal redundancy. **Low Motion:*** **More Pixels and More Frames:** Temporal tricks and dithering shine at high framerate and resolution. **More Pixels and More Frames:*** **Blur:** Rough reflections and depth of field need more rays or more texture samples, but you can use a lower resolution for raytracing and texture lookups. **Blur:**
#### Some Quick Tips
* One texture + different vertex colors = many looks. * If your floating point data is only positive, put data in the sign bit. * If float data is always < 1, there's a free bit in the exponent. * The least significant bit of a float probably doesn't matter. Yolo. * Float precision issues are often skill issues. Use [Herbie](https://herbie.uwplse.org/) and floatfloat methods before giving up and using larger types. * Decouple framerates. Shadows, gi, terrain and entities don't all need to update at the same frequency. * Quantize vertex positions * Paletting * Checkerboarded chroma * Lower lods = fewer animation frames needed * Velocity buffers: 2D and a low bitrate are often enough * Normalized vectors can be stored in 2D * Only impostors facing the camera need to be loaded. (Overpowered) * Learn about sparse bit octrees/contrees
* One texture + different vertex colors = many looks. * If your floating point data is only positive, put data in the sign bit. * If float data is always < 1, there's a free bit in the exponent. * The least significant bit of a float probably doesn't matter. Yolo. * Float precision issues are often skill issues. Use [Herbie](https://herbie.uwplse.org/) and floatfloat methods before giving up and using larger types. [Herbie](https://herbie.uwplse.org/)* Decouple framerates. Shadows, gi, terrain and entities don't all need to update at the same frequency. gi* Quantize vertex positions * Paletting * Checkerboarded chroma * Lower lods = fewer animation frames needed lod* Velocity buffers: 2D and a low bitrate are often enough * Normalized vectors can be stored in 2D * Only impostors facing the camera need to be loaded. (Overpowered) * Learn about sparse bit octrees/contrees
item.dc:creator: Jodie

item.title: Wake
item.description:
th
![](https://static2.mtlws.ca/wake.png)
![](https://static2.mtlws.ca/wake.png)
Your words linger in the silence. Eyes wet, throat dry, I felt the cold wind blow, and I broke like a wave.
In the wake of my deeds, am I the only one? or did you feel the same cold wind as you sailed away?
item.dc:creator: Jodie

item.title: Fast Approximate |Length| and Squaring²
item.dc:creator: Jodie

item.title: RSS Feed
item.description:
th
My blog now has an RSS feed! You can find it [here](/feed.xml).
[here](/feed.xml)
The script to generate the feed is my first experience trusting ai to write all the code. The recently released Claude 3.7 is pretty good so far. I found out today that GitHub Copilot allows you to use it right in your ide.
aiide
item.dc:creator: Jodie

item.title: Productivity and Happiness are the Same
item.description:
th
The idea of work-life balance is harmful.
#### Work isn't Hard
It's tragic that we accept work is unfun. We shouldn't accept that we're forced to do something that sucks. This framing isn't just bad for you, it makes workers as a group accept terrible working conditions. This shouldn't be considered “normal”.
#### Fun isn't Easy
Most people's idea of fun is spending money and engaging in vice. People see fun as synonymous with being counter-productive. You're expected to continuously destroy what you build in an endless cycle of consumption that never leads to your life improving.
#### Work-Life Maximisation
Productivity and happiness aren't opposing forces that need to be balanced. They aren't at odds with each other. Work can be fun, and leisure can be productive. An activity can be more fun _and_ more productive. What you want isn't "balance". You want work-life _maximisation_.
_and__maximisation_
item.dc:creator: Jodie

item.title: Story
item.description:
th
![](https://static2.mtlws.ca/story.jpg)
![](https://static2.mtlws.ca/story.jpg)
there's a voicemail full of excuses you haven't listened to yet
I wonder if you miss me like I do if you'll rewind the tape to hear my voice
your scarf still hangs by my door waiting for you to walk in
I watch the snow fall outside my window I trace your name in the condensation
I don't think I feel sad anymore our story ended before it got old
item.dc:creator: Jodie

item.title: Don't Quit Your Vices
item.description:
th
Replace unhealthy sugary treats with fancy salads. Replace social media by hanging out with your friends or texting them. Replace gambling with starting a business. Vices usually fill a void, find out a healthy way of filling it.
item.dc:creator: Jodie

item.title: Modern Color Utility Classes
item.dc:creator: Jodie

item.title: Fast Sigmoid (Updated)
item.description:
th
[https://godbolt.org/z/djTqa5x7K](https://godbolt.org/z/djTqa5x7K)
[https://godbolt.org/z/djTqa5x7K](https://godbolt.org/z/djTqa5x7K)``` `static float garbage_sigmoid(float x){ return utf( ~ftu( utf(~ftu(x))- utf(ftu(4.f)|ftu(x)&ftu(-0.f)) ) ); }`
``` `static float garbage_sigmoid(float x){ return utf( ~ftu( utf(~ftu(x))- utf(ftu(4.f)|ftu(x)&ftu(-0.f)) ) ); }`
item.dc:creator: Jodie

item.title: Still
item.description:
th
time moves like honey sweet and slow
even the wind limps dragging its hem across the thorns
the clouds weep softly too heavy for the sky to hold
each moment settles like dust remembered but never recalled
here, for a moment or an eternity I can stop doing, and simply be
item.dc:creator: Jodie

item.title: Stop Having Goals
item.description:
th
Humans aren't made to have long-term goals. If you have a goal, don't think about the goal. Stop caring about the goal. Failure to achieve the goal is neutral. Do not let yourself think about it. Not having reached the goal yet should never be on your mind.
Learn to enjoy the process. Want to be strong? Learn to enjoy exercise. Want to lose weight? Learn to enjoy hunger. Want to be rich? Learn to enjoy saving money. Want to make friends? Learn to enjoy being interested in people. Do it without an agenda.
Do the process every day, prioritise it, make it a part of your schedule. Get good at the process.
#### But…
Ask yourself if the process is still serving you. Maybe you're already past the goal.
item.dc:creator: Jodie

item.title: Do you really enjoy this or are you addicted?
item.description:
th_really_
Things that are pleasurable aren't necessarily addictive, and things that are addictive aren't necessarily pleasurable.
Addiction is a trickster. It makes you rationalize. You need this! You enjoy this! Without those stories, you'd feel guilty and stupid. But addiction isn't pleasurable. Addiction is a cycle of craving (unpleasant), a short hit of meaningless pleasure (optional), followed by a sense of emptiness (also unpleasant).
Many fun things aren't addictive at all. Nobody gets addicted to taking a walk or eating a delicious salad. Meanwhile people get addicted to alcohol, picking their scabs and checking their ex's Instagram.
item.dc:creator: Jodie

item.title: King of the Hill
item.description:
![](https://static2.mtlws.ca/hill.jpg)
![](https://static2.mtlws.ca/hill.jpg)rd
shimmering dewdrops jewels in the grass
dawn's vaulted ceiling adorned with golden clouds
this hill, my ornate throne where I am king
item.dc:creator: Jodie

item.title: The Emotional Cyborg
item.description:
st
One limitation of therapy is that you can't have a therapist be with you 24/7 to examine your behavior. Luckily we now have ai that can monitor everything you say, and correct bad thought patterns and behaviours in real time. Computers turned us into information and math cyborgs, now they allow us to enhance our emotional intelligence.
ai
item.dc:creator: Jodie

item.title: Furniture Free
item.description:
nd
I recently got interviewed by Charlotte Collins of Architectural Digest about furniture-free living. Read the article [here](https://www.architecturaldigest.com/story/extreme-minimalism-people-who-pared-all-the-way-down-talk-living-nearly-furniture-free).
[here](https://www.architecturaldigest.com/story/extreme-minimalism-people-who-pared-all-the-way-down-talk-living-nearly-furniture-free)
item.dc:creator: Jodie

item.title: A Programmer Without a Computer
item.description:
th
I've been using my phone as my main computer for a week now. I'm even coding on it, and it's… fine?
I'm writing this blog post on my phone right now in fact, using termux, neovim and unexpected keyboard.
![](4-neovim.png)
![](4-neovim.png)
I might get a foldable phone and/or a physical keyboard in the future, but I'm not in a rush.
item.dc:creator: Jodie

item.title: The Most Ergonomic Desk is No Desk At All
item.description:
th
About a year back, I moved into a new apartment and couldn't bring my desk. I could work at the kitchen table or on the sofa in the meantime. My plan was quickly thwarted by my new roommate's cat, who I would come to learn, was the most annoying cat in the world.
Having no money for a desk at the time, I sat on the floor in my room, and after a short adjustment period, I found that I had a new superpower. I could sit on the floor with no back support, and it was fine.
After a few months of this, I decided to buy a desk, and I found that I couldn't sit at it for more than a few hours without my back hurting. So I went back to sitting on the floor, and later donated the desk.
I haven't bought any furniture since, and I don't plan to. People didn't have furniture for most of human history, and many people in the third world still don't. This isn't as weird as it sounds.
item.dc:creator: Jodie

item.title: Get Off Your Phone
item.description:
th
Do you feel like you have no time? No energy? Check your screen time in your phone settings. Mine was _eight_! If you had asked me to estimate, I would have said 2! I had no idea! That's a full-time job!
_eight_
#### How I Regained Control
Here's what allowed me to cut my screen time down to 1-2 hours a day:
1. I started sharing my screen time with my friends every day to keep myself accountable 2. I removed time wasting apps entirely 3. I even removed my browser and replaced it with Firefox Focus which doesn't keep you logged in and deletes your tabs when you open it again. No logins, no algorithmic content. 4. I added the screen time widget to my home screen 5. Apps like Instagram allow you to make widgets to take you directly to direct messages, or you can use an app like Beeper 6. Silent notifications! I don't feel like checking the app, but it also doesn't distract me by buzzing 7. I don't check my phone first thing in the morning, I get up and work 8. I try to smell the flowers and enjoy being bored
* I started sharing my screen time with my friends every day to keep myself accountable * I removed time wasting apps entirely * I even removed my browser and replaced it with Firefox Focus which doesn't keep you logged in and deletes your tabs when you open it again. No logins, no algorithmic content. * I added the screen time widget to my home screen * Apps like Instagram allow you to make widgets to take you directly to direct messages, or you can use an app like Beeper * Silent notifications! I don't feel like checking the app, but it also doesn't distract me by buzzing * I don't check my phone first thing in the morning, I get up and work * I try to smell the flowers and enjoy being bored
item.dc:creator: Jodie

item.title: Should I Buy the Thing? A Flowchart
item.description: th``` ┌───────────────┐ │ Am I happy? │ └┬─────────────┬┘ Yes No │ ┌───┴─────────────┐ │ │ Will the thing │ │ │ make me happy? │ You don't need └───┬──────────┬──┘ the thing Yes No to be happy. │ │ │ │ │ Don't buy Buy the Don't buy the thing. thing. the thing. ```
item.dc:creator: Jodie

item.title: Bit Arrays for Fast Graph Search
item.description:
th
One common flaw in [HNSW](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hierarchical_navigable_small_world) implementations is using ordered sets to track visited neighbors. This causes a huge performance overhead.
[HNSW](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hierarchical_navigable_small_world)
Using a bit array to keep track of visited neighbors is much faster.
For a code example, see [Joann](https://github.com/MathGeniusJodie/joann), my HNSW implementation.
[Joann](https://github.com/MathGeniusJodie/joann)HNSW
item.dc:creator: Jodie
